Syllabus Index (Custom Numbering)
I. Paper I: Economics, Money and Banking
I.A Khanda (A): Economics
I.A.1 National Income Accounting
I.A.1.1 GDP Concepts & Measurements
I.A.1.2 GDP Computation Challenges in Nepal Frameworks
I.A.1.3 GNP, Investment & Macroeconomic Indicators
I.A.2 Growth Theories
I.A.2.1 Harrod-Domar Formulation
I.A.2.2 Solow-Swan Steady-State Model
I.A.2.3 New Classical Model & Implication Matrix
I.A.3 Price & Output System Dynamics
I.A.3.1 Perfect Competition Systems
I.A.3.2 Imperfect, Monopolistic & Oligopoly Market Structures
I.A.4 International Trade Infrastructure
I.A.4.1 Theories of International Trade and Investment
I.A.4.2 Foreign Trade & Investment Policies of Nepal
I.A.4.3 Balance of Payment (BOP) Dynamics
I.A.4.4 Globalization Impacts on Nepalese Financial Systems
I.A.5 Development Economics
I.A.5.1 Structure and Characteristics of Developing Economies
I.A.5.2 Determinants of Economic Growth & Structural Challenges
I.A.5.3 Privatization Present Affairs in Nepal
I.A.6 Development Planning
I.A.6.1 Frameworks and Core Importance of Planning
I.A.6.2 Development Planning in Nepal: Issues & Implementation Challenges
I.A.7 Policy Frameworks
I.A.7.1 Monetary Policy Architecture
I.A.7.2 Fiscal Policy and Coordinated Stabilizations
I.A.7.3 Commercial, Industrial, Tourism & Foreign Investment Policies
I.A.8 Reforms & Sectoral Matrices
I.A.8.1 Financial Sector Reform Programme Achievements & Appraisal
I.A.8.2 Sectoral Analysis: Agriculture, Industry, Trade & Tourism
I.B Khanda (B): Money and Banking
I.B.1 Banking Systems
I.B.1.1 History, Roles and Core Functions of Commercial & Central Banks
I.B.1.2 Management of Commercial Banks: Profitability vs Liquidity
I.B.1.3 Assets and Liability Management (ALM) Operational Frameworks
I.B.1.4 Lending Principles and Credit Portfolio Controls
I.B.1.5 Determination Matrix of Interest Rates & Base Spread Rules
I.B.2 Strategic Operations
I.B.2.1 Bank Marketing: Products, Instruments & Customer Line Segmentation
I.B.2.2 Marketing Strategies & Conversion Routing Architecture
I.B.2.3 Foreign Exchange Management: Policy, Practices and Regulations in Nepal
I.B.2.4 Money Supply Dynamics: Definitions, High Power Money & Multipliers
I.B.2.5 Inflation: Concepts, Causes, Measurements & Phillips Curve Dynamics
I.B.3 Risk & Consolidation Engineering
I.B.3.1 Banking Risks Analysis and Risk Management Practices
I.B.3.2 Mergers, Amalgamations, Acquisitions & Takeover Frameworks
I.B.3.3 Merger & Acquisition System Failures & Nepalese Realities
I.B.4 Financial Intelligence & AML
I.B.4.1 AML/CFT Concepts, Processes & Operational Vulnerabilities
I.B.4.2 Identification, Analysis and Reporting of Threshold & Suspicious Transactions (STR/TTR)
I.B.4.3 Asset (Money) Laundering Prevention Act, 2064 Frameworks
I.B.5 Legislative & Regulatory Compendiums
I.B.5.1 The Constitution of Nepal Context
I.B.5.2 Banks and Financial Institutions Act (BAFIA), 2073
I.B.5.3 Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058 Statutory Matrix
I.B.5.4 Banking Offence and Punishment Act, 2064
I.B.5.5 Payment and Settlement Act, 2075
I.B.5.6 Recovery of Debt of Banks and Financial Institutions Act, 2058
I.B.5.7 Secured Transaction Act, 2063 & Foreign Exchange Regulation Act, 2019
I.B.5.8 Securities Act, 2063, Income Tax Act, 2058 & Regulations
I.B.6 NRB Unified Directives Engine
I.B.6.1 Directives 1-5: Capital Adequacy (Basel III) & Liquidity Controls
I.B.6.2 Directive 2: Asset Classification & 1% Pass Loan Loss Provisioning Matrix
I.B.6.3 Directives 6-10: Risk Management Protocols & Corporate Governance Rules
I.B.6.4 Directives 11-21: Consumer Protection, Specialized Sector Lending Targets
II. Paper II: Management and Finance
II.A Khanda (A): Management
II.A.1 Organization Development (OD)
II.A.1.1 Organization Theories, Architectural Evolution & Models
II.A.1.2 OD Processes at Group & Inter-Group Framework Tiers
II.A.1.3 Transforming and Changing Organizational Cultures
II.A.2 Strategic Planning Structures
II.A.2.1 Environmental Scanning Methods & PESTEL Configurations
II.A.2.2 SWOT Analysis Matrices & Strategy Formulation Systems
II.A.2.3 Strategic Implementation, Evaluation & Operational Control Loops
II.A.3 Management Fundamentals
II.A.3.1 Core Meaning, Theories, Principles and System Functions
II.A.3.2 Emerging Modern Concepts & Shifting Executive Management Roles
II.A.3.3 Organizational Leadership Theories and Transformational Matrices
II.A.4 Human Resource Engineering
II.A.4.1 Approaches to Strategic HR Planning & Job Description Architecture
II.A.4.2 Recruitment, Selection, Integration and Systems Onboarding
II.A.4.3 Training, Development Methodologies & ROI Analytics Metrics
II.A.4.4 Performance Management Systems, Balance Scorecards & Compensation
II.A.4.5 Labor Relations, Bargaining, Dispute Negotiations & Union Realities
II.A.4.6 Contemporary Strategic HR Challenges and Impediments in Nepalese Banking
II.A.5 Organizational Behavior (OB)
II.A.5.1 Fundamentals of OB, Group Dynamics & Operational Team Formation
II.A.5.2 Motivation Theories, Corporate Communication Flows & Friction Points
II.A.5.3 Time Management Systems, Conflict Management & Decision-Making Models
II.A.6 Quality Systems & Controls
II.A.6.1 Total Quality Management (TQM) Techniques & Kaizen Adaptations
II.A.6.2 Benchmarking Frameworks & Quality Assurance Implementations
II.A.6.3 Managing Structural Institutional Transitions & Growth Paradigms
II.A.7 Governance & Responsibility Matrix
II.A.7.1 Corporate Governance Frameworks, Ethics, Morality & Integrity Systems
II.A.7.2 Social Responsibility Mandates & Fiduciary Transparency Boundaries
II.A.8 IT Architecture & Security Management
II.A.8.1 IT/MIS Concepts, Scope & E-Commerce Operational Infrastructures
II.A.8.2 Hardware, Operating System and DBMS Architecture Controls
II.A.8.3 IT-Risk Analysis Frameworks, Security Auditing & Access Governance
II.A.8.4 Cyber Crime Controls, IT Policies & Legislative Frameworks in Nepal
II.A.8.5 NRB IT Policy and Technical Guidelines Implementation Manuals
II.B Khanda (B): Finance
II.B.1 Financial System Architecture
II.B.1.1 Financial Management, Systems, Capital Markets & Money Markets
II.B.1.2 Securities Market Mechanics & Derivative Instruments Configuration
II.B.1.3 Financial Planning, Capital Cost Analysis & Investment Appraisals
II.B.2 Capital Budgeting Strategies
II.B.2.1 Capital Budgeting Valuation Techniques & Asset Selection Profiles
II.B.2.2 Mutually Exclusive Proposals Analysis & Variable Lifecycle Options
II.B.2.3 Pay Back Method Adjustments & Strategic Capital Rationing Vectors
II.B.3 Reporting & Disclosure Engineering
II.B.3.1 Cash Flow Analysis Concepts, Operating, Investing & Financing Tracking
II.B.3.2 Nepal Financial Reporting Standards (NFRS) Structural Framework
II.B.3.3 International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Harmonization Paths
II.B.3.4 NFRS 9 Financial Instruments Asset Classification Matrix Rules
II.B.3.5 Expected Credit Loss (ECL) Modeling for Forward-Looking System Risk
II.B.4 Portfolio & Profit Optimizations
II.B.4.1 Portfolio Management Analysis, Selection Mechanics & Efficient Frontiers
II.B.4.2 Associated Portfolio Structural Risk Matrix and Asset Pricing Models
II.B.4.3 Profit Management: CVP Analysis, Cost-Volume Configurations & Breaks
II.B.4.4 Sources and Uses of Funds Analytics, Linear & Goal Programming Models
II.B.5 Advanced Project Appraisals
II.B.5.1 Techniques and Core Engineering Methods of Project Evaluation
II.B.5.2 Analysis of Financial Statements, Horizontal/Vertical Ratios & DuPont Matrix

Day 1 16th May 2026 (2/2 Done)

🌅 Morning Session (2 Hours) - I.A.1.1 (✅ Done)
🔹 Toggle View
EXAM EXECUTION CONTROL:
Paper I Khanda (A) National Income Accounting I.A.1.1 GDP Concepts & Measurements

First Pass Evaluation: Build core theoretical paradigms, extract structural financial trends, and link applicable directives frameworks.

1
2
3
4
A. Foundation & Introduction
Gross Domestic Product (GDP) acts as the primary scorecard of a nation's economic health, representing the total monetary value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
B. Definitions of Terms
  • Nominal GDP: Economic output evaluated at current market prices, unadjusted for inflation.
  • Real GDP: Output adjusted for price changes (inflation/deflation), reflecting true growth.
  • GDP Deflator: A measure of the level of prices of all new, domestically produced, final goods and services.
C. Flow Chart / Mind Map Overview
🧠 MindMap Overview:
[Output Method (Agriculture, Industry, Services)] ➜ [Income Method (Wages, Rent, Interest, Profit)] ➜ [Expenditure Method (C + I + G + NX)] ➜ National GDP
D. History & Development
Developed by Simon Kuznets in the 1930s to help the US government understand the Great Depression, GDP was adopted globally post-Bretton Woods (1944). In Nepal, systematic national accounting began in the 1960s with the establishment of the Central Bureau of Statistics (now NSO).
E. Objective, Need & Significance
To quantify the absolute size of the economy, determine the trajectory of growth, and formulate foundational monetary and fiscal policies.
F. Core Content (25-Mark Depth)
The Core Construct: Nepal primarily relies on the Value Added (Production) approach for structural compilation due to data constraints in the expenditure model. The economy is bifurcated into 18 ISIC economic activities. A critical shift in understanding Nepal's GDP is its heavy reliance on the tertiary (service) sector, which contributes over 60%, while agriculture contributes roughly 24% despite employing a vast majority. The challenge is the 'informal economy' paradox, which operates outside the recorded GDP metric but drives real-world consumption.
G. Calculation / Derivation Structure
Derivation (Expenditure Approach):
Y = C + I + G + (X - M)
Where C = Consumption, I = Investment, G = Gov Expenditure, X = Exports, M = Imports. In Nepal, (X - M) represents a massive deficit gap.
H. Positive & Negative Aspects
Strong Aspect: Provides a globally standardized baseline for IMF/WB evaluations and sovereign ratings.
Weakness: Ignores non-market transactions (household work), environmental degradation, and crucially for Nepal, Remittance is NOT included in GDP.
I. Recent Developments & Contemporary Issues
Rebasing of the National Accounts base year to 2010/11 by the National Statistics Office (NSO), capturing newer digital economy segments and better categorization of the service sector.
J. Relation to Banking Sector (National/International)
Banking Nexus: Nominal GDP directly anchors credit expansion limits. A standard prudential rule is that broad money (M2) and credit growth should align with Nominal GDP growth to prevent systemic asset bubbles. NRB uses projected GDP to calibrate the Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) and Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR).
K. Real World Example / Story
The Remittance Paradox: Bikash, consider a family in Jhapa receiving Rs. 50,000 from Qatar. This money is deposited in NBL, creating banking liquidity. The family buys imported Indian electronics. Result: GDP does not increase (remittance is a transfer, imports reduce net exports). Yet, banking deposits swell and consumption rises. This disconnect is why GDP alone fails to capture Nepal's actual banking liquidity cycle.
L. Quantitative Data (NRB, MOF, WB)
Nepal GDP (2023/24 Est): Rs. 5.70 Trillion. Targeted Growth: 6.0% (Monetary Policy 2080/81). Inflation Target: 6.5%. (Source: MOF/NRB).
M. Policy, Act, Directives & Regulatory Provisions
Regulatory Anchor: Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058 (Section 4) mandates the NRB to formulate monetary policy that sustains macroeconomic stability to support the Government's GDP growth targets.
N. Current Situation & Problems in Banking
1. High Credit-to-GDP Ratio: Nepal's private sector credit-to-GDP exceeds 90%, yet economic growth is stagnant (low credit productivity).
2. Informal Sector Leakage: An estimated 35-40% of the economy is unbanked/shadow, skewing GDP accuracy.
O. Nepal Bank Limited (NBL) Strategy & Implementation
NBL Strategic Adoption (Level 9 Directive): Nepal Bank Limited must integrate MOF's GDP sectoral growth targets directly into the Annual Credit Policy. By aligning lending portfolios toward high-GDP-contributing sectors (like Energy and Tourism) while rigorously enforcing NFRS 9 Expected Credit Loss (ECL) modeling—using macro-variables like GDP growth as forward-looking indicators—NBL can optimize risk-weighted assets. Furthermore, strictly adhering to the 1% Pass Loan Loss Provisioning (Directive 2/2082) ensures capital buffer integrity against macroeconomic shocks.

First Pass Evaluation: Build core theoretical paradigms, extract structural financial trends, and link applicable directives frameworks.

A. Foundation & Introduction
Gross Domestic Product (GDP) acts as the primary scorecard of a nation's economic health, representing the total monetary value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
B. Definitions of Terms
  • Nominal GDP: Economic output evaluated at current market prices, unadjusted for inflation.
  • Real GDP: Output adjusted for price changes (inflation/deflation), reflecting true growth.
  • GDP Deflator: A measure of the level of prices of all new, domestically produced, final goods and services.
C. Flow Chart / Mind Map Overview
🧠 MindMap Overview:
[Output Method (Agriculture, Industry, Services)] ➜ [Income Method (Wages, Rent, Interest, Profit)] ➜ [Expenditure Method (C + I + G + NX)] ➜ National GDP
D. History & Development
Developed by Simon Kuznets in the 1930s to help the US government understand the Great Depression, GDP was adopted globally post-Bretton Woods (1944). In Nepal, systematic national accounting began in the 1960s with the establishment of the Central Bureau of Statistics (now NSO).
E. Objective, Need & Significance
To quantify the absolute size of the economy, determine the trajectory of growth, and formulate foundational monetary and fiscal policies.
F. Core Content (25-Mark Depth)
The Core Construct: Nepal primarily relies on the Value Added (Production) approach for structural compilation due to data constraints in the expenditure model. The economy is bifurcated into 18 ISIC economic activities. A critical shift in understanding Nepal's GDP is its heavy reliance on the tertiary (service) sector, which contributes over 60%, while agriculture contributes roughly 24% despite employing a vast majority. The challenge is the 'informal economy' paradox, which operates outside the recorded GDP metric but drives real-world consumption.
G. Calculation / Derivation Structure
Derivation (Expenditure Approach):
Y = C + I + G + (X - M)
Where C = Consumption, I = Investment, G = Gov Expenditure, X = Exports, M = Imports. In Nepal, (X - M) represents a massive deficit gap.
H. Positive & Negative Aspects
Strong Aspect: Provides a globally standardized baseline for IMF/WB evaluations and sovereign ratings.
Weakness: Ignores non-market transactions (household work), environmental degradation, and crucially for Nepal, Remittance is NOT included in GDP.
I. Recent Developments & Contemporary Issues

J. Relation to Banking Sector (National/International)
Banking Nexus: Nominal GDP directly anchors credit expansion limits. A standard prudential rule is that broad money (M2) and credit growth should align with Nominal GDP growth to prevent systemic asset bubbles. NRB uses projected GDP to calibrate the Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR) and Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR).
K. Real World Example / Story
The Remittance Paradox: Bikash, consider a family in Jhapa receiving Rs. 50,000 from Qatar. This money is deposited in NBL, creating banking liquidity. The family buys imported Indian electronics. Result: GDP does not increase (remittance is a transfer, imports reduce net exports). Yet, banking deposits swell and consumption rises. This disconnect is why GDP alone fails to capture Nepal's actual banking liquidity cycle.
L. Quantitative Data (NRB, MOF, WB)
Nepal GDP (2023/24 Est): Rs. 5.70 Trillion. Targeted Growth: 6.0% (Monetary Policy 2080/81). Inflation Target: 6.5%. (Source: MOF/NRB).
M. Policy, Act, Directives & Regulatory Provisions
Regulatory Anchor: Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058 (Section 4) mandates the NRB to formulate monetary policy that sustains macroeconomic stability to support the Government's GDP growth targets.
N. Current Situation & Problems in Banking
1. High Credit-to-GDP Ratio: Nepal's private sector credit-to-GDP exceeds 90%, yet economic growth is stagnant (low credit productivity).
2. Informal Sector Leakage: An estimated 35-40% of the economy is unbanked/shadow, skewing GDP accuracy.
O. Nepal Bank Limited (NBL) Strategy & Implementation
NBL Strategic Adoption (Level 9 Directive): Nepal Bank Limited must integrate MOF's GDP sectoral growth targets directly into the Annual Credit Policy. By aligning lending portfolios toward high-GDP-contributing sectors (like Energy and Tourism) while rigorously enforcing NFRS 9 Expected Credit Loss (ECL) modeling—using macro-variables like GDP growth as forward-looking indicators—NBL can optimize risk-weighted assets. Furthermore, strictly adhering to the 1% Pass Loan Loss Provisioning (Directive 2/2082) ensures capital buffer integrity against macroeconomic shocks.
